title: Factors of polynomials of low individual degree
speaker: Rafael Oliveira
abstract: In [kal89], Kaltofen proved the remarkable fact that multivariate polynomial factorization can be done efficiently, in randomized polynomial time. Still, more than twenty years after Kaltofen's work, many questions remain unanswered regarding the complexity aspects of polynomial factorization, such as the question of whether factors of polynomials efficiently computed by arithmetic formulas also have small arithmetic formulas, asked in [KSS14], and the question of bounding the depth of the circuits computing the factors of a polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic-circuit factorization problem and depth gap
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up arithmetic circuits, recalls Kaltofen-style closure of small circuits under factoring, and formulates the unresolved structural questions about depth, sparsity, and formula size of factors.

proof_stages.label: Bounded-individual-degree theorem and root-factor precursor
proof_stages.summary: Bounded individual degree is introduced as a tractable generalization of multilinearity, the main depth-preserving factor theorem is stated, and the earlier Dvir-Spielman-Yehudayoff type result for factors y-g(x) is positioned as the root case to be generalized.

proof_stages.label: Hensel lifting for separated linear roots
proof_stages.summary: For a monic polynomial that splits as separated linear factors in y, successive homogeneous parts of the roots are recovered by Hensel/Newton lifting, with black-box interpolation extracting homogeneous components without expanding all coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Approximate roots for monic nonsplit factors
proof_stages.summary: When the monic polynomial need not split over the base field, roots in the algebraic closure are replaced by low-degree Taylor-like approximate roots, and a Euclidean-division argument shows their product approximates the irreducible factor.

proof_stages.label: Reversal to avoid recursive leading-coefficient blow-up
proof_stages.summary: For the nonmonic case, the naive induction on variables would repeatedly enlarge the circuit while extracting leading coefficients, so reversal swaps constant and leading coefficients and moves the size blow-up outside the recursive term.

proof_stages.label: Rational-function monic reduction without computing inverses
proof_stages.summary: The reversed polynomial is analyzed as a rational-function multiple of a monic polynomial modulo high-degree terms, using truncated inversion only analytically so that the constructed circuit depth is not increased by explicitly computing the inverse.

proof_stages.label: Inductive assembly of the factor circuit
proof_stages.summary: Reversal, rational monic reduction, approximate-root products, and induction on the constant coefficient of the factor are combined to obtain a depth d+4 approximate factor with product top gate and then a depth d+5 exact factor by interpolation.

proof_stages.label: Algorithmic consequences, framework, and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recaps the randomized output-size algorithm, explains the uses of PIT and resultants, abstracts the proof as a reduction from general low-depth factorization to low-depth approximate roots, and lists derandomization and degree-dependence open questions.

examples.name: Finite-field Frobenius sparse-polynomial counterexample
examples.note: Over characteristic p, a sparse p-th power such as x_1^p+...+x_n^p factors through the Frobenius as a power of a linear form, showing that naive sparsity preservation fails over finite fields and motivating the characteristic-zero caveat.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs a sparse polynomial over �f_p and uses Frobenius to exhibit and discuss a nonsparse factor.

examples.name: Cyclotomic factor of x^p-1 over the rationals
examples.note: In Q[x], the sparse polynomial x^p-1 has the dense irreducible cyclotomic factor 1+x+...+x^{p-1}, illustrating a known degree-dependent sparsity blow-up.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker factors x^p-1 over the rationals into the cyclotomic factor x^{p-1}+...+1 and uses it to compute a sparsity blow-up.
